hints.junior: Every fetch that can be cancelled should have an AbortController. The three-line pattern (create, pass signal, abort on cleanup) prevents an entire category of bugs.
hints.mid: AbortError is expected, not an error. The catch block pattern: if AbortError, return null; else re-throw. Keep it consistent across your data layer.
hints.staff: One signal can cancel multiple parallel fetches. Pass the same AbortController.signal to all fetch calls in a Promise.all: one abort() drops them all.

hints.junior: Start a request and cancel it in broken mode: it completes anyway. Fixed mode shows the cancelled request in the Network panel with an actual termination.
hints.mid: The Network panel strikethrough is the proof: the browser actually dropped the TCP connection, not just ignored the response. That's real bandwidth saved.
hints.staff: In production, cancelled search requests reduce server load significantly, especially for typeahead endpoints that fire 10+ requests per search session.

hints.junior: When you call abort(), the fetch doesn't error quietly — it rejects with a DOMException named AbortError, and catching that specific name and returning early is the whole pattern.
hints.mid: One AbortController.signal can cancel a whole Promise.all of parallel fetches at once. Pass the same signal to every call in the batch, and a single abort() drops all of them together.
hints.staff: Build named cancellation into your fetch wrapper at the service layer — cancel every 'search' request without touching an in-flight 'user-profile' request. TanStack Query already gives you this at the hook level via its per-query AbortSignal; SWR doesn't cancel the in-flight request the same way, only the stale response, so the service pattern is what covers SWR, non-React, or custom fetch layers.

hints.junior: Always check err.name === 'AbortError' and return early instead of re-throwing — re-throwing it fires your global error handler for a cancellation that was completely intentional.
hints.mid: Debounce and abort solve different problems: debounce cuts how often you fire a request, abort stops a stale one from corrupting state after it lands. Use both — they're not substitutes for each other.
hints.staff: In tab-based or wizard-style UIs, named cancellation lets navigating away cancel only the data load for the tab you left — a blanket cancel-everything approach would kill requests the next tab still needs.

anecdotes.junior: My first AbortController use was a product detail page where navigating away left a 2-second fetch running and crashing the next page's state. Three lines fixed it.
anecdotes.mid: The 'can't update state on unmounted component' React warning is almost always a fetch that needs an AbortController in the cleanup function.
anecdotes.staff: Teams that use TanStack Query or SWR correctly get AbortController for free: the libraries pass the signal. The bugs appear when teams write custom fetch hooks without it.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're learning how to cancel fetch requests, so they don't update state after you navigate away or start a new request.
sections.mid: This case is the networking deep-dive on AbortController: the three-line pattern, AbortError handling, and composing signals across parallel requests.
sections.staff: We're covering the full signal API, parallel abort patterns, framework integration (TanStack Query, SWR), and the production bandwidth case for cancellation.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle. Read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: Start a request and cancel it in broken mode: it completes anyway. Fixed mode shows the cancelled request in the Network panel with an actual termination.
sections.mid: The Network panel strikethrough is the proof: the browser actually dropped the TCP connection, not just ignored the response. That's real bandwidth saved.
sections.staff: In production, cancelled search requests reduce server load significantly, especially for typeahead endpoints that fire 10+ requests per search session.
